column8: Percentage of children aged 0–59 months whose height-for-age Z-score (HAZ) < -2 standard deviations (SD) from the World Health Organization Child Growth Standards.
column9: Stunting indicates chronic dietary inadequacy and poor diet quality over time, often linked to limited access to diverse and nutrient-rich foods. It is a key outcome of food systems that fail to provide sustainable, diverse, and nutritious diets, especially during the first 1,000 days of life.
column13: (Number of children 0–59 months with HAZ < -2 SD / Total number of children measured) × 100

column8: Percentage of children aged 0–59 months whose weight-for-height Z-score (WHZ) < -2 SD from the World Health Organization Child Growth Standards.
column9: Wasting reflects acute undernutrition and short-term food insecurity. It is highly sensitive to shocks in the food system (e.g., price spikes, seasonal shortages), thus serving as a critical indicator of dietary adequacy, access, and resilience. Reducing wasting aligns with ensuring affordable and stable access to nutritious foods.
column13: (Number of children 0–59 months with WHZ < -2 SD / Total number of children measured) × 100

column8: Percentage of children aged 0–59 months whose weight-for-age Z-score (WAZ) < -2 SD from WHO standards.
column9: Underweight captures both chronic and acute undernutrition, making it a composite indicator of overall child nutritional status. It reflects deficiencies in diet quantity and quality, as well as broader food system performance in delivering sufficient and affordable food.
column13: (Number of children 0–59 months with WAZ < -2 SD / Total number of children measured) × 100

column7: Prevalence of obesity and overweight for under 5
column8: Percentage of children aged 0–59 months whose weight-for-height Z-score (WHZ) > +2 SD from WHO standards.
column9: Child overweight reflects early exposure to energy-dense, nutrient-poor diets, often driven by shifts in food environments and market systems. It signals the nutrition transition and highlights imbalances in food systems where cheap, unhealthy foods are more accessible than healthy options.
column13: (Number of children 0–59 months with WHZ > +2 SD / Total number of children measured) × 100

column8: Percentage of women aged 15–49 years with Body Mass Index (BMI) < 18.5 kg/m².
column9: Maternal underweight indicates insufficient dietary intake and poor food access, affecting women’s health and intergenerational nutrition outcomes. It reflects inequities in food affordability and intra-household food distribution, and is critical for assessing the inclusiveness of food systems.
column13: (Number of women with BMI < 18.5 / Total number of women measured) × 100

column7: Prevalence of obesity and overweight for women
column8: Asia standard - IDI & WPRO: Proportion of adults aged 18 and older with BMI ≥ 23 kg/m². Estimates are age-standardized
column9: Overweight and obesity among women are key indicators of diet-related non-communicable disease (NCD) risk and unhealthy food environments. They reflect increased consumption of ultra-processed and energy-dense foods, often associated with food systems that prioritize low cost over nutritional quality and sustainability.
column13: (Number of women with BMI ≥ 23 / Total number of women measured) × 100

column8: Spatial accessibility to food outlets based on walking distance from population clusters
column9: Shows how easily residents can reach food outlets on foot, helping identify spatial barriers to healthy food access.
column13: Calculated by measuring walkable distance from population locations to the nearest food outlets within a defined buffer
column15: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column8: Spatial distribution of total number of healthy food outlets per commune
column9: Reveals the availability of protective food environments across communes.
column13: Calculated as the number of healthy outlets divided by the area (km²) of each commune.
column15: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column8: Spatial distribution of total number of unhealthy food outlets per commune
column9: Identifies areas where obesogenic food options are concentrated.
column13: Calculated as the number of unhealthy outlets divided by the area (km²) of each commune.
column15: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column8: Spatial distribution of total number of mixed food outlets per commune
column9: Captures environments where healthy and unhealthy options coexist, highlighting ambiguous food landscapes.
column13: Calculated as the number of mixed outlets divided by the area (km²) of each commune.
column15: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column8: Balance between unhealthy and healthy outlets per commune, indicating pressure levels
column9: Summarizes the balance between unhealthy and healthy outlets, pinpointing communes under greatest obesogenic pressure.
column13: Calculated by dividing the number of unhealthy outlets by the number of healthy outlets in each commune.
column15: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column8: Proportion of residents who can reach healthy outlets within a walkable buffer
column9: Shows the share of residents with walkable access to protective food environments.
column13: Calculated as the proportion of the population living within the walkable buffer of healthy outlets
column15: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column8: Proportion of residents who can reach unhealthy outlets within a walkable buffer
column9: Indicates population exposure to obesogenic food environments within walkable distances.
column13: Calculated as the proportion of the population living within the walkable buffer of unhealthy outlets
column15: Updates depend on the availability of new OpenStreetMap data and revised population estimates.

column7: Rice flow estimations from Vietnam regions to Hanoi, by rural and urban areas, in tons per year (2010-2022)

column8: Agricultural climate resilience measures how well cropland vegetation maintains healthy vegetation condition during agricultural drought events.
column9: Captures production regions most vulnerable to water balance deficits that would propagate to supply chain shocks.
column12: Internally produced from ERA5-Land and MODIS
column13: Calculated using the vegetation condition index and the SPEI6 medium-term agricultural drought classification system to assess vegetation condition relative to drought severity.
column16: Land cover classification accuracy of active cropland

column8: Measures total terrestrial water storage (including groundwater and soil moisture) and detects seasonal anomalies
column9: Water availability is critical for planning agricultural intensification interventions as regions vulnerable to water shortages are less resilient to climate change.
column13: Calculated by removing the climatology and seasonality from the signal to reveal only anomalies from the usual seasonal water cycle.
column15: Can only update when CSR updates

column8: Displays the frequency of natural disasters occuring at national scale retrieved from the EMDAT database of reports
column9: Quantifies historical biophysical shock exposure

column8: A composite index measuring the resilience of urban food commodity price systems, combining two components: GARCH(1,1) volatility persistence (the proportion of a price shock that carries forward into the following month) and shock frequency (the proportion of months in which prices exceeded a crisis-level threshold, defined as the 95th percentile of the pooled cross-commodity absolute log-return distribution). Higher scores indicate greater resilience.
column9: Urban food security in low-income cities is determined not only by whether food prices are high but by whether price shocks are transient or permanent. The UFPRI captures the structural self-stabilising capacity of city-region food markets, a dimension of fragility that household-level food security indicators do not measure. It enables cross-commodity comparison of price system resilience within the Addis Ababa food basket and supports evidence-based targeting of market infrastructure investment.
column10: Unitless (0-1) Dimensionless composite score, normalised 0–1 within commodity group (0 = least resilient, 1 = most resilient)
column12: World Food Programme Vulnerability Analysis and Mapping (WFP VAM) database monthly wholesale food prices, 120 Ethiopian markets
column13: GARCH(1,1) models estimated on monthly log-return series (rₜ = ln(Pₜ/Pₜ₋₁)) using an ARMA(1,1) mean equation via the rugarch package in R. Persistence component = 1 − (α₁ + β₁). Shock frequency component = 1 − (proportion of months exceeding 95th percentile cross-commodity threshold). Both components normalised to [0,1] within commodity group. UFPRI = arithmetic mean of the two normalised components.
column16: Pulse UFPRI scores are preliminary due to short data windows (19–20 observations); shock frequency component is unreliable for pulses and driven entirely by persistence. Cereal and pulse scores are not directly comparable (normalised separately).

column8: Reduced to district level, this indicator shows the percentage area occupied by different land-cover categories, from aquaculture to urban
column9: Land-cover identifies which areas are supported by urban/peri-urban production as a buffer to produce import shocks

column8: Proportion of adults aged 18 and older with diabetes. Diabetes is defined as having a fasting glucose of 7.0 mmol/L or higher, being on medication for raised blood glucose, or having a past diagnosis of diabetes. Estimates are age-standardized.
column9: Adult diabetes reflects long-term exposure to unhealthy dietary patterns, particularly high consumption of refined carbohydrates, added sugars, and ultra-processed foods. It signals structural issues in the food system where nutritious diets are less accessible or affordable than energy-dense alternatives. This indicator is critical for assessing the role of food environments in driving diet-related non-communicable diseases (NCDs).

column8: Proportion of adults aged 18 and older with hypertension. Hypertention is defined as blood pressure, systolic, and/or diastolic blood pressure ≥140/90 mmHg. Estimates are age-standardized.
column9: Hypertension is strongly associated with diets high in salt, processed foods, and low in fruits and vegetables, reflecting imbalances in food availability and consumption patterns. It highlights gaps in food systems that fail to support healthy dietary choices, and underscores the need for interventions promoting affordable access to diverse, nutrient-rich foods and healthier food environments.

column8: No. of food poinsoning cases
column9: Food poisoning reflects the safety dimension of the food system, indicating failures in food handling, storage, processing, and regulation. It directly affects consumer trust and the utilization of available food, and can limit access to safe and nutritious diets. Ensuring food safety is essential for achieving sustainable and reliable food systems, particularly in informal markets common in low- and middle-income settings.
